title: Capstone: Word Frequency Counter
description: Build a text analyzer that tokenizes a paragraph, counts word frequency, and prints the top words — combining strings, loops, dicts, sorting, and functions.
objectives: Split and normalize text into tokens (lowercasing, punctuation stripping)
Sort a dictionary by value and break ties alphabetically
Decompose a program into small, named functions

tests.name: tokenize handles ellipsis and multiple marks

recap: You tokenized text, counted with a dict, and sorted by value with a tiebreaker — the same pattern used in search engines, log analysis, and any "what's most common?" question.
practicePrompts: Add support for stop-word filtering (skip common words like 'the', 'and', 'a').
Count sentences too — split on '.', '!', '?' and print the sentence count.
Print the least-frequent word instead of the most-frequent.

practiceExercises.title: Longest word
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `longest(words)` that returns the longest word in the list. If two words are tied for length, return the one that appears FIRST in the list. The grader will test it with several lists.
practiceExercises.goal: Iterate a list, track the longest item seen so far.
hints: Track a `best` variable — start it as the first word.
Use `>` (strictly greater) so the first word of a tie stays as the winner.

practiceExercises.title: Count unique words
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `count_unique(text)` that returns the number of distinct words in `text` (case-insensitive). The grader will test it with several sentences.
practiceExercises.goal: Normalize case, then count distinct items with a set or dict.

tests.name: basic sentence with repeats
tests.call: count_unique('The cat and the dog and the bird')

practiceExercises.title: Most frequent word
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `most_frequent(words)` that returns a tuple `(word, count)` for the most-frequent word in the list. If counts tie, pick the word that comes first alphabetically. The grader will test it with several lists.
practiceExercises.goal: Build a frequency dict, then scan for the max — with an alphabetical tiebreaker.

hints: Tiebreak: `if count > best_count or (count == best_count and word < best_word):`
